---
title: "DATA SCIENCE BOOTCAMP 2026"
subtitle: "PART 1: R"
author:
  - name: Martin Ho
    email: martinkh.ho@mail.utoronto.ca
date: "2026-07-15"
date-format: long
bibliography: references.bib
csl: american-medical-association.csl
notes-after-punctuation: true
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: solarized
    css: slides.css
    chalkboard: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.08
    transition: fade
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    navigation-mode: linear
    controls: true
    controls-auto: false
    controls-layout: edges
    link-external-newwindow: true
    footer: "Data Science Bootcamp 2026 · Part 1: R"
    logo: assets/images/r_logo.png
---


## What you will learn

By the end of this session, you should be able to:

- Explain why code improves **reproducibility**
- Navigate **RStudio** and organize work with an **R project**
- Distinguish types, classes, and data structures
- Read and write basic R code using conditions, loops, and functions


## Declaration of AI-assisted technologies

All instructional material in this presentation was written by the author. OpenAI Codex (GPT-5.6-Sol, reasoning: extra high) helped proofread and format the slide deck. The author reviewed, edited, and verified the suggestions as needed and takes full responsibility for the final product.


## Why write code?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Manual workflow

- Mistakes are easy to miss
- Repeating work costs time
- Steps may be undocumented
- Workflow is hard to reproduce
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
### Scripted workflow

- Rules are reusable and reviewable
- Changes can be rerun consistently
- Every transformation is recorded
- Workflow is easier to reproduce
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
Code turns analyses from a sequence of clicks into an **auditable recipe**.
:::

::: {.notes}
- Think of how you would perform analyses on Excel
- Typos, missingness, duplicates, formatting errors
- Merge, filter, create/remove/transform columns
:::


## Why R?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="66%"}
- Free and open source
- Designed for statistics and data analysis
- Strong data processing and visualization
- Interactive: create, inspect, and transform objects
- Extended by packages maintained by a global community
:::

::: {.column width="28%"}
![](assets/images/r_logo.png){fig-alt="R logo" width="78%"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
- A programming language + an interactive environment
- Very popular in biostatistics + epidemiology
- Everything in R is an object
- Objects are stored in memory → can run and tweak as you go
:::


## Two software philosophies [@kenneth2023sas]

+---------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| SAS                                                                 | R                                                                                                                         |
+=====================================================================+===========================================================================================================================+
| Proprietary and paid                                                | Open source and free                                                                                                      |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Centralized validation, documentation, and support by vendor        | Decentralized community-developed ecosystem of packages (validation, quality, and support may vary)                       |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Standardized procedures                                             | Flexible and extensible                                                                                                   |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Mature regulated workflows                                          | Increasing adoption in regulated workflows                                                                                |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="28%"}
![](assets/images/sas_logo.png){fig-alt="SAS logo" width="50%"}
:::

::: {.column width="66%"}
SAS historically dominates regulatory drug submissions and pharmacoepidemiology.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
- **Python:** gaining popularity for ML (but is designed as general-purpose + PyPI generally less strict than CRAN)
  - *FYI Python philosophy: "There should be one — and preferably only one — obvious way to do it."*
- **LLM support:** code + documentation
:::


## The regulatory ecosystem is moving

- Pharmaverse coordinates open-source tools for clinical reporting [@eugenio2023pharmaverse]
- Novo Nordisk completed an R-based new drug application in 2021 [@appsilonteam2024why]
- FDA expanded accepted R-related file formats in 2025 after industry–FDA collaboration [@rconsortium2025expanded; @rconsortium2026submissions]

::: {.key-point}
The transition requires **training, infrastructure, governance, and reproducible validation evidence**.
:::

::: {.notes}
- **Pharmaverse** (2020) regulatory-compliant R packages: instead of duplicated silos → standardized workflows
- **Pharmacoepidemiology:** similar motivations, e.g., ICES infrastructure + macros
- Shift will take time
:::


## R is interactive

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
The **console** evaluates expressions immediately:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
#| code-line-numbers: "|1|1-2|1-3|1-4"
x <- 1
y <- 2
z <- x + y
z
```

::: {.fragment}
**Scripts** preserve the sequence of commands so the analysis can be rerun, reviewed, and changed.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.fragment}
![](assets/images/r_console.png){fig-alt="Base R console" width="88%"}
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
- The basic R app is not pretty!
- A number of quality of life improvements when using RStudio
:::


## Why RStudio?

RStudio is an integrated development environment for R [@positrstudio].

<div class="rstudio-pane-cycle">
  <img class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="1" src="assets/images/rstudio_gui.png" alt="RStudio interface showing its four panes">
  <img class="fragment fade-in-then-out" data-fragment-index="1" src="assets/images/rstudio_console.png" alt="RStudio console pane">
  <img class="fragment fade-in-then-out" data-fragment-index="2" src="assets/images/rstudio_editor.png" alt="RStudio editor pane">
  <img class="fragment fade-in-then-out" data-fragment-index="3" src="assets/images/rstudio_env.png" alt="RStudio Environment and History pane">
  <img class="fragment" data-fragment-index="4" src="assets/images/rstudio_other.png" alt="RStudio Files, Plots, Packages, Help, and Viewer pane">
</div>

::: {.notes}
- **Console:** write ad hoc commands (work is lost when R session ends)
- **Editor:** write reusable scripts (work to be saved)
- **Environment:** inspect objects in memory
- **Other:** browse files, view plots, manage packages, view help, and more
:::


## R projects give your repositories a home

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
An `.Rproj` file:

- Establishes an initial project working directory
- Encourages portable relative paths
- Stores project-specific IDE settings
- Has its own history (`.Rhistory`)
:::

::: {.column .project-tree-column width="40%"}
```text
.
├── data/
├── analysis/
├── output/
├── docs/
└── project.Rproj
```
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
Relative paths like `./folder/file.csv` are much preferred over user-specific absolute paths like `/Users/your-name/Desktop/folder/file.csv`.
:::

::: {.notes}
- Absolute paths are sensitive to OS, username, and wherever they decide to save the repository → irreproducible
- Also integrates with Git and `renv` but we will discuss these in Part 2
:::


## Packages extend R

- Packages bundle code, documentation, and sometimes data
- Base R comes with packages already
- But base R may not be most efficient, flexible, or readable
- Install custom packages maintained by global community from CRAN [@rcoreteam2026writing; @crancontributed].

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Install once

```r
install.packages("readr")
```
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
### Load each session

```r
library(readr)

# Or call one function
readr::read_csv("data.csv")
```
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
- External packages are not always better:
  - Learning curve
  - Dependencies
  - May be slower
  - May require permissions
  - Textbooks may only use base R
:::


## The Tidyverse

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="68%"}
A very popular collection of packages that share common data representations and design conventions:

- `readr` — import data
- `dplyr` — transform data
- `tidyr` — reshape data
- `ggplot2` — visualize data

`library(tidyverse)` loads the core packages [@wickhamdata].
:::

::: {.column width="26%"}
![](assets/images/tidyverse_logo.png){fig-alt="Tidyverse logo" width="92%"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
- Can be more efficient and readable than base R
- R4DS
:::


## Core operators

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
+----------------+---------------------------------------------+
| Purpose        | Operators                                   |
+================+=============================================+
| Assignment     | `<-`                                        |
+----------------+---------------------------------------------+
| Arithmetic     | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`, `sqrt()`, `log()`  |
+----------------+---------------------------------------------+
| Relational     | `>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`, `==`, `!=`\           |
|                | `%in%`                                      |
+----------------+---------------------------------------------+
| Logical        | `&`, `|`, `!`                               |
+----------------+---------------------------------------------+
:::

::: {.column width="43%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
x <- 1
x + 2
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
x > 2
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
x %in% c(1, 2, 3)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
x > 0 & x < 2
```
:::
:::

Object names may contain letters, digits, periods, and underscores. Use *snake_case* (e.g., `my_variable_name`) [@wickhamdata]. Names cannot start with a digit or underscore, nor a period followed by a digit [@ethzurichquotes].

::: {.notes}
- What is `x`? It is 1, not 3! Unless you assign it, such as `x <- x + 2`, it will not save.
:::


## Encode decisions with conditions

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
#| code-line-numbers: "|1|1-4|1-6|1-9"
cases <- 75

if (cases > 100) {
  print("Emergency")
} else if (cases > 50) {
  print("Warning")
} else {
  print("Caution")
}
```

<br>

::: {.key-point}
Conditions make analytical decisions explicit and reviewable.
:::

::: {.notes}
- The second expression is only evaluated if the first is false, therefore to have reached the second expression, cases must be <= 100.
- Can be a single `if`
- Can have no `else if`
- Can have multiple `else if`
- Can have no `else`
:::


## Repeat decisions with loops

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
cases <- c(25, 75, 125)

for (x in cases) {
  if (x > 100) {
    print("Emergency")
  } else if (x > 50) {
    print("Warning")
  } else {
    print("Caution")
  }
}
```
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
cases <- 125

while (cases >= 0) {
  if (cases > 100) {
    print("Emergency")
  } else if (cases > 50) {
    print("Warning")
  } else {
    print("Caution")
  }
  cases <- cases - 25
}
```
:::
:::

- `for` loops iterate over a sequence
- `while` loops continue while a condition remains true.


## Objects and function calls

> To understand computations in R, two slogans are helpful:
>
> - Everything that exists is an object.
> - Everything that happens is a function call.

— John M. Chambers [@chambers2014objectoriented]

::: {.notes}
- Numbers, text, functions, and even packages are all objects
- Objects are manipulated by function calls (assignment, arithmetic, etc. - and custom functions)
:::


## Type, class, and structure

+----------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------+
| Concept        | Question             | Examples                                            |
+================+======================+=====================================================+
| **Type**       | How is it stored?    | double, integer, logical, character, list           |
+----------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------+
| **Class**      | How does it behave?  | numeric, date, logical, character, list, data frame |
+----------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------+
| **Structure**  | How is it organized? | vector, matrix, array, list, data frame             |
+----------------+----------------------+-----------------------------------------------------+

<br>

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
typeof(1)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
typeof(as.character(1))
```
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
typeof(as.Date("2026-07-15"))
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
class(as.Date("2026-07-15"))
```
:::
:::


## Common R data structures

![Data structures in R [@grolemundhandson]](assets/images/r_datastructures.png){fig-alt="Diagram of common R data structures" width="48%"}


## Vectors, lists, and data frames

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="32%"}
### Vector

One atomic type:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
my_vector <- c("1", "2", "3")
my_vector
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
my_vector[2]
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
my_vector <- 0:6
my_vector
```
:::

::: {.column width="32%"}
::: {.fragment}
### List

Arbitrary objects:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
my_list <- list("A", c(1, 2))
my_list
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
my_list[2]
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
my_list[[2]]
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="32%"}
::: {.fragment}
### Data frame

Named list of equal-length vectors:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
my_df <- data.frame(
  year = c(2020, 2021),
  cases = c(25, 125)
)
my_df
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
my_df[,2]
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
my_df$cases
```
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
- **Data frame:** each column is a vector that can in turn be indexed
:::


## Functions make work reusable

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| output-location: fragment
classify_cases <- function(cases) {
  response <- if (cases > 100) {
    "Emergency"
  } else if (cases > 50) {
    "Warning"
  } else {
    "Caution"
  }
  return(response)
}

classify_cases(75)
```

::: {.key-point}
Functions turn a sequence of commands into a reusable tool.
:::

::: {.notes}
- Functions return values, but can also have intentional side effects (e.g., printing to console, plotting a graph, writing to file)
:::



## From files to findings[@wickhamdata]

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Read data] --> B[Clean]
  B --> C
  subgraph analysis[" "]
    direction LR
    C[Transform] --> D[Explore]
    D --> E[Model]
    E --> C
  end
  analysis --> F[Communicate]
  style analysis fill:#eeeeee,stroke:none
```

<hr class="slide-divider">

::: {.fragment}
Common file types include `.csv`, `.xlsx`, `sas7bdat`, `.rds`, and `.RData`.

::: {.file-type-icons}
::: {.file-type-icon}
![](assets/images/csv_icon.png){fig-alt="CSV file icon"}
:::
::: {.file-type-icon}
![](assets/images/excel_icon.png){fig-alt="Microsoft Excel logo"}
:::
::: {.file-type-icon}
![](assets/images/sas_logo.png){fig-alt="SAS logo"}
:::
::: {.file-type-icon}
![](assets/images/r_logo.png){fig-alt="R logo"}
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
- Data scientists spend most of their time on data wrangling - not modeling
- Cleaning: typos, missingness, duplicates, formatting
- Transforming: merge, filter, create/remove/transform columns
:::


## R for ICES projects

- ICES is the institution that stores administrative health data in Ontario, Canada
- It has well-established SAS infrastructure and macros
- R is supported but you need to apply for access to the Analytic Grid Pilot program

::: {.notes}
- R on RAE is incredibly outdated
- AGP has relatively limited RAM though
:::


## Reproducibility habits

- Keep raw data separate from processed data
- Use relative paths
- Load only the packages that you need
- Record R and package versions
- Never rely on an old `.RData` workspace to reconstruct an analysis

::: {.key-point}
A reproducible workflow records **data, code, versions, and decisions**.
:::


## Key takeaways

1. R makes data workflows explicit, reusable, and inspectable.
2. RStudio and R projects organize the work around your scripts.
3. Packages extend R—but introduce dependencies that require control.
4. Objects have types, classes, and structures.
5. Conditions, loops, and functions turn decisions into reusable code.


## Further readings

- R for Data Science (2e) [@wickhamdata]
- Hands-On Programming with R [@grolemundhandson]
- Object-Oriented Programming, Functional Programming and R [@chambers2014objectoriented]

::: {.notes}
- See book for more details, such as updating packages
:::


## References {#refs}

<script>
window.addEventListener("load", function () {
  function fitReferenceList() {
    const isPrintView = window.location.search.includes("print-pdf");
    const slide = isPrintView
      ? document.getElementById("refs")
      : Reveal.getCurrentSlide();
    if (!slide || slide.id !== "refs") return;

    const references = slide.querySelector(":scope > .references");
    if (!references) return;

    // Start at the CSS size, then reduce only the bibliography if needed.
    references.style.fontSize = "";
    const maximum = parseFloat(window.getComputedStyle(references).fontSize);
    const minimum = 10;
    const slideBox = slide.getBoundingClientRect();
    const referencesBox = references.getBoundingClientRect();
    const availableHeight = slideBox.bottom - referencesBox.top - 8;

    if (referencesBox.height <= availableHeight) return;

    let lower = minimum;
    let upper = maximum;
    for (let iteration = 0; iteration < 12; iteration += 1) {
      const size = (lower + upper) / 2;
      references.style.fontSize = `${size}px`;
      if (references.getBoundingClientRect().height <= availableHeight) {
        lower = size;
      } else {
        upper = size;
      }
    }
    references.style.fontSize = `${lower}px`;
  }

  fitReferenceList();
  Reveal.on("ready", fitReferenceList);
  Reveal.on("slidechanged", function (event) {
    if (event.currentSlide.id === "refs") {
      window.requestAnimationFrame(fitReferenceList);
    }
  });
  window.addEventListener("resize", function () {
    window.requestAnimationFrame(fitReferenceList);
  });
});
</script>
